content.text: Let me look at the main DataGrid.razor.cs file which contains the grouping logic:
Now let me read the key method `GroupDisplayData()` more closely:

Now I can see the bug. The `GroupDisplayData()` method on line 1049-1067 only uses `groupableColumns[0]` (the first groupable column) to create the initial grouping. It does NOT call `RecursiveGroup()` to apply subsequent levels of grouping. The `RecursiveGroup` method exists (line 1109) but is never called from `GroupDisplayData()`.

Let me verify this by searching for where `RecursiveGroup` is called:

content.content:
<tool_use_error>InputValidationError: Grep failed due to the following issue:
The required parameter `pattern` is missing</tool_use_error>

1109:    private void RecursiveGroup( int iteration, List<GroupContext<TItem>> oldGroupedData, List<GroupContext<TItem>> newGroupedData )
1127:                RecursiveGroup( iteration + 1, (List<GroupContext<TItem>>)oldGroup?.NestedGroup, nestedGroup );

tool_use_result.content:
1109:    private void RecursiveGroup( int iteration, List<GroupContext<TItem>> oldGroupedData, List<GroupContext<TItem>> newGroupedData )
1127:                RecursiveGroup( iteration + 1, (List<GroupContext<TItem>>)oldGroup?.NestedGroup, nestedGroup );

content.text:
Confirmed. `RecursiveGroup` is only called recursively within itself (line 1127) but is never called from `GroupDisplayData()` or any other method. The `GroupDisplayData()` method only groups by the first column and never applies the remaining grouping levels.

Let me also look at the `GroupContext` class to understand it

                        Field="@nameof( Employee.Name )"
                        Caption="Name"

@code {
    [Parameter] public EventCallback<DataGridGroupingChangedEventArgs<Employee>> GroupingChanged { get; set; }

    [Parameter]
    public IEnumerable<Employee> Data { get; set; } = new List<Employee>()
    {
        new() { Name = "John", Department = "Engineering", City = "Zagreb" },
        new() { Name = "Sarah", Department = "Sales", City = "Rijeka" },
        new() { Name = "Ana", Department = "Engineering", City = "Split" },

    public class Employee
    {
        public string Name { get; set; }

        public string Department { get; set; }

        public string City { get; set; }
    }
}

content.text:
Now I understand the bug clearly:

In `GroupDisplayData()` (line 1041), when `GroupBy` is null, the code only groups by `groupableColumns[0]` (the first column) and never calls `RecursiveGroup()` to nest subsequent group levels. The `RecursiveGroup()` method already exists and is properly implemented but is never invoked.

The fix should: After creating the first-level groups, call `RecursiveGroup()` with `iteration: 1` to apply remaining grouping levels.

Let me first write a test to verify the current behavior, then apply the fix.

Let me look at the test structure to understand how they work

content.content: File unchanged since last read. The content from the earlier Read tool_result in this conversation is still current — refer to that instead of re-reading.

I see - `DisplayGroupedData` checks `dirtyView`. The problem might be that `dirtyView` was already consumed by a previous `DisplayData` access. Let me check the render tree to verify if the grouping happens correctly and also check if there's a caching issue

I see - `DisplayGroupedData` does NOT reset `dirtyView` after calling `GroupDisplayData`, but `DisplayData` does reset it. So accessing `DisplayGroupedData` first calls `GroupDisplayData` which depends on `DisplayData` (which resets `dirtyView`). The issue is that `DisplayGroupedData` accesses `DisplayData` which sets `dirtyView = false`, and then `GroupDisplayData` doesn't set it back. But actually, `DisplayGroupedData` checks `dirtyView` which was just set to `false` by `DisplayData` accessed from within `GroupDisplayData`.

Wait, let me re-read the `GroupDisplayData` flow more carefully. Actually, `GroupDisplayData` accesses `DisplayData` which will call `FilterViewData()` if `dirtyView` is true, and then set `dirtyView = false`. After that, `DisplayGroupedData` checks `dirtyView` which is now `false`, so if it was already computed once, it won't recompute. But during the first access, it should work...

OK, the code looks correct. The issue must be that `dirtyView` is being reset. But wait, looking more carefully at `DisplayGroupedData`:

```csharp
if ( dirtyView )
    GroupDisplayData();
```

It only calls `GroupDisplayData()` if `dirtyView` is true. But the problem is: when `GroupDisplayData()` is called, it accesses `DisplayData` internally (e.g., via `DisplayData.GroupBy(...)`), and `DisplayData` resets `dirtyView = false`. But since `GroupDisplayData` is already executing, the `dirtyView` reset by `DisplayData` shouldn't matter for the current call.

<DataGrid TItem="Employee"
          Data="@Data"
          Groupable
          ShowGrouping
          GroupingChanged="GroupingChanged">
    <DataGridColumns>
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.Name )"
                        Caption="Name"
                        Groupable />
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.Department )"
                        Caption="Department"
                        Groupable />
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.City )"
                        Caption="City" />
    </DataGridColumns>
</DataGrid>

@code {
    [Parameter] public EventCallback<DataGridGroupingChangedEventArgs<Employee>> GroupingChanged { get; set; }

    [Parameter]
    public IEnumerable<Employee> Data { get; set; } = new List<Employee>()
    {
        new() { Name = "John", Department = "Engineering", City = "Zagreb" },
        new() { Name = "Sarah", Department = "Sales", City = "Rijeka" },
        new() { Name = "Ana", Department = "Engineering", City = "Split" },
    };

    public class Employee
    {
        public string Name { get; set; }

        public string Department { get; set; }

        public string City { get; set; }
    }
}

tool_use_result.originalFile:
<DataGrid TItem="Employee"
          Data="@Data"
          Groupable
          ShowGrouping
          GroupingChanged="GroupingChanged">
    <DataGridColumns>
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.Name )"
                        Caption="Name"
                        Groupable />
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.Department )"
                        Caption="Department"
                        Groupable />
        <DataGridColumn TItem="Employee"
                        Field="@nameof( Employee.City )"
                        Caption="City"
                        Groupable />
    </DataGridColumns>
</DataGrid>

@code {
    [Parameter] public EventCallback<DataGridGroupingChangedEventArgs<Employee>> GroupingChanged { get; set; }

    [Parameter]
    public IEnumerable<Employee> Data { get; set; } = new List<Employee>()
    {
        new() { Name = "John", Department = "Engineering", City = "Zagreb" },
        new() { Name = "Sarah", Department = "Sales", City = "Rijeka" },
        new() { Name = "Ana", Department = "Engineering", City = "Split" },
    };

    public class Employee
    {
        public string Name { get; set; }

        public string Department { get; set; }

        public string City { get; set; }
    }
}
